title: Local-Global Principles and Effective Rates of Equidistribution For Semisimple Orbits
speaker: Andreas Wieser
abstract:
We prove an effective equidistribution theorem for semisimple
closed orbits on compact adelic quotients. The obtained error depends polynomially on the minimal complexity of intermediate orbits and the complexity of the ambient space. As an application, we establish a local-global principle for representations of quadratic forms, improving the codimension assumptions and providing effective bounds in a theorem of Ellenberg and Venkatesh. This is joint work with Manfred Einsiedler, Elon Lindenstrauss, and Amir Mohammadi.

proof_stages.label: Integral representation problem and local obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates representation of one integral quadratic form by another via an integral linear embedding, defines the corresponding p-adic and real local representations, and poses the local-global question.

proof_stages.label: Positive-definite local-global theorem to be proved effectively
proof_stages.summary: After recalling earlier codimension bounds for positive-definite forms, the speaker states the new codimension-three/four result under squarefree and large-minimum hypotheses, with a stronger asymptotic counting statement and power-saving error in the background.

proof_stages.label: Semisimple-period equidistribution framework
proof_stages.summary: The arithmetic application is reframed in terms of periodic orbits of semisimple subgroups on adelic homogeneous spaces, recalling the qualitative Mozes-Shah/Ratner-type limit theorem and introducing the need for an effective rate measured by minimal intermediate complexity.

proof_stages.label: Uniform effective equidistribution theorem
proof_stages.summary: For connected semisimple groups H inside G inside SL_N and shifted H-periods in an adelic quotient, the theorem bounds the discrepancy between averaging over the period and the ambient orbit by a negative power of the minimal complexity of intermediate orbits, with explicit dependence on level and Sobolev norms and constants uniform enough for induction.

proof_stages.label: Position relative to prior effective equidistribution results
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains how earlier real and adelic results typically required finite centralizer or maximality hypotheses, highlighting the centralizer obstruction and the novelty of treating the more general semisimple-period setting needed here.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from quadratic forms to adelic orbit hitting
proof_stages.summary: Using the spin or orthogonal group of the large form, local representability and the spin genus produce a small real orbit inside a component of an adelic quotient, and effective equidistribution turns the desired integral representation into a quantitative hitting problem whose only relevant intermediate obstructions are controlled by the minimum of the quadratic form.

proof_stages.label: Producing almost-invariant displacements by p-adic shearing
proof_stages.summary: The proof chooses a prime where the period has noncompact dynamics, embeds an SL_2 in the p-adic stabilizer, finds nearby transverse points by volume/recurrence, uses unipotent shearing and an effective closing lemma to obtain group elements that almost preserve the period measure, with height-dependent inequalities made explicit.

proof_stages.label: Controlled algebraic enlargement for the induction
proof_stages.summary: The new almost-symmetry is converted, after a controlled perturbation, into a genuine enlargement of the acting group while avoiding dimension-dropping generation pathologies, enabling the inductive equidistribution argument.

examples.name: One-variable representation by a quadratic form
examples.note: The case k=1 is used to make the definition concrete: representing the small form amounts to representing an integer by the large quadratic form, while larger k prescribes lengths and angles of several vectors.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the k=1 case only to say it amounts to representing an integer by the larger quadratic form, without carrying out a representation or computation.

examples.name: Indefinite quadratic forms as an easier comparison
examples.note: The indefinite case is mentioned to contrast with the positive-definite setting, since strong approximation allows the codimension threshold to be pushed lower.
examples.engagement_reason: Indefinite forms are invoked as an easier comparison where codimension can be pushed lower via strong approximation, but no specific indefinite form is analyzed.

examples.name: Rational line in a torus
examples.note: A rational line containing lower-complexity rational subtori shows why the effective rate should be governed by the minimum covolume or complexity of intermediate rational subspaces.
examples.engagement_reason: The rational line in a torus is used as an illustrative analogy for effective equidistribution rates and rational subspaces, with the Fourier argument only cited as an exercise.

passes.reason: A rational line is a standard torus suborbit, invoked to show intermediate rational subtori can obstruct naive equidistribution rates.
A rational line is an ordinary periodic torus orbit, used as a toy obstruction to naive equidistribution rates depending only on the orbit's own complexity.
The rational torus line is used as an obstruction to naive equidistribution rates, since it may lie in a lower-complexity intermediate rational subspace.
